text: The surgeon leaves the lateral adhesions intact while visually identifying the left ureter.
The vermiculation of the bowel is visible, showing rhythmic contractions along the intestinal wall.
The surgeon verifies the ureter's normal anatomical position before reducing the lateral attachments to mobilize the sigmoid colon.
The surgeon extends the mesenteric dissection distally towards the rectum, continuing the mobilization of the sigmoid colon.
The surgeon identifies the estimated point of transection at the coalescence of the colonic tinea, preparing for the next step in the procedure.
The surgeon creates a tunnel beneath the colon, visually separating it from underlying structures.
The surgeon prepares to transect the mesentery, a step taken before dividing the colon, with visible attention to the surrounding anatomical structures.
The robotic third arm is used to facilitate exposure, providing a clear view of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealing device to dissect the mesentery proximally, progressing until reaching the disease-free segment of the colon.
The surgeon mobilizes the left colon, extending its length to facilitate the proximal colon's reach into the pelvis.
The surgeon injects indocyanine green intravenously and activates the fluorescence lens of the da Vinci robot, providing a visual map of the colonic blood supply.
The surgeon uses the robotic system to accurately identify the point of transection on the colon, with clear visualization of the targeted area.
The surgeon performs the distal transection of the colon, utilizing two staple loads to complete the cut.
The surgeon uses scissors to complete the proximal transection if one staple load is insufficient, preparing for the removal of the staple line in the subsequent step.
The surgeon has completely freed the specimen, which is now set aside, indicating the completion of the mobilization and transection process.
The surgeon discards the removed staple line into the colonic lumen after completing the transection.
The surgeon removes the pre-placed suture from the anvil and positions the anvil into the proximal colon, preparing for the next step in the procedure.
The surgeon creates a purse string suture at the colonic opening, preparing the site for anastomosis.
The surgeon inserts the EEA stapler into the rectum and deploys the spike, preparing for the anastomosis.
The surgeon joins the stapling device with the anvil, creating an anastomosis between the rectum and proximal colon.
The surgeon occludes the colon proximal to the anastomosis and fills the pelvis with saline to perform a leak test, checking for any visible leaks at the anastomosis site.
The surgeon over-sews the staple line with several silk interrupted sutures, reinforcing the anastomosis regardless of the leak test results.
The surgeon removes the needles and suture material from the surgical site, followed by the extraction of the specimen from the operative field.
